items.correct: Proteins denaturing and bonding to form a solid structure when heated.
items.misconception: The egg shell breaking when boiled.
items.why: Students think coagulation is something physical happening to the shell. It's PROTEIN CHEMISTRY: heat unfolds the proteins, which then bond together, turning liquid egg into solid - what makes a fried egg set.

items.correct: Acidic conditions (low pH) prevent most bacteria from growing.
items.misconception: The vinegar kills the food cells.
items.why: Students think pickling poisons bacteria. It works by creating an environment INHOSPITABLE to most microbes - low pH stops them growing, even if they're present.

items.q: What is the difference between a food allergy and an intolerance?
items.correct: An allergy involves the immune system; intolerance does not.
items.misconception: They are the same with different severity.
items.random_wrong: Allergies are temporary, intolerances are permanent.
items.why: Students treat these as severity points on one spectrum. They have different mechanisms - allergies trigger immune (IgE) responses (potentially fatal anaphylaxis); intolerances are digestive (e.g., lactase deficiency).
The answer examiners want is: India. “France” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Wild yeast starter. “Baking powder” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many bread varieties are listed in this lesson?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
